text: The surgeon places permanent and absorbable V-lock sutures in the surgical field, securing the mesh and tissue layers.
Upon entry and docking, the surgeon begins stripping the peritoneum, removing the thin membrane layer to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon ensures that the coated macroporous mesh is positioned directly against the fascia, emphasizing the importance of proper placement.
The surgeon dissects the fat associated with the falciform ligament, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects peri-umbilical fat away from the defect, creating a clear landing zone for mesh placement.
The surgeon prepares to close a relatively small defect, opting for a transverse closure technique.
The surgeon uses a permanent V-lock suture to close the defect, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon performs a straightforward closure of the defect using a V-lock suture, running the suture to lock it in place.
The surgeon runs the V-lock suture back on itself to secure it in place, completing the closure of the defect.
After closing the defect, the surgeon positions the mesh over the surgical site, ensuring it covers the area adequately.
The surgeon displays the mesh that was positioned at the beginning of the procedure.
The surgeon places a stitch in the central portion of the mesh to secure and position it within the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh positioning and identifies the need for additional preperitoneal dissection to ensure proper placement.
The surgeon ensures the mesh lays flat against the fascia, smoothing it out to achieve proper positioning after defect closure.
The surgeon uses absorbable V-lock sutures to fixate the mesh, running the sutures from one side of the mesh to the other to secure it in place.
In the background, the surgeon uses a needle from the fascial closure to tack up the mesh, securing it in place against the fascia.
The surgeon secures the mesh against the abdominal wall using a circumferential baseball stitch, ensuring the mesh remains flat and stable.
The surgeon secures the intraperitoneal mesh, ensuring the barbs are exposed while laying flat against the fascia to prevent adhesions.
The surgeon completes the circumferential fixation of the mesh against the abdominal wall using a baseball stitch technique.
A ten-centimeter mesh is visible, secured against the abdominal wall with circumferential fixation.
The surgeon completes the circumferential fixation of the mesh, ensuring it is securely positioned in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the circumferential fixation of the mesh, showing the final placement and appearance of the mesh at the end of the procedure.
